question: What is the difference between struct and class in Swift?
answer:
• struct — value type. Copied on assignment. No inheritance. Free memberwise initializer. Mutating methods need `mutating` keyword. Stack-allocated when small.
• class — reference type. Shared by reference. Supports inheritance and deinit. Identity (===) works. Heap-allocated, ARC-managed.

Use struct by default. Reach for class when you need: identity (the same instance referenced by many owners), inheritance, AnyObject conformance for Objective-C interop, or deinit.

question: What are protocols in Swift and how are they different from interfaces?
answer:
A protocol declares a contract — methods, properties, associated types, initializers — that conforming types must implement.

Differences vs Java/C# interfaces:
• Both classes AND value types (struct, enum) can conform.
• Protocols can have associated types — generic-like type parameters (`associatedtype Item`).
• Protocol extensions can provide default implementations and add methods to all conformers without modifying their source.
• Existentials (`any Protocol`) are runtime-erased boxes; opaque types (`some Protocol`) are compile-time concrete.

Protocol-oriented programming (POP) is Swift's idiomatic answer to inheritance.

question: Explain enums with associated values and raw values in Swift.
answer:
Swift enums are first-class types with two powerful extras:

• Raw values — every case has the same underlying primitive (Int, String, Float). Useful for serialization, network constants. Auto-conform to RawRepresentable.
• Associated values — each case carries its own typed payload. Lets you model algebraic data types — perfect for representing screen state, Result, parser tokens, etc.

Enums are value types and can have methods, computed properties, and protocol conformances. Combine with `switch` for exhaustive pattern matching.

question: What are extensions in Swift and what can/can't they do?
answer:
Extensions add functionality to an existing type — your own types, stdlib types, even imported framework types — without subclassing.

Can add:
• Computed properties (instance + static)
• Methods (instance + static + mutating)
• Initializers (convenience for classes)
• Subscripts
• Protocol conformance
• Nested types

Cannot add:
• Stored properties (no slot in the original type's memory layout)
• Override existing methods (extensions add, not replace)
• Non-final designated initializer for classes

Idiomatic uses: split a type by feature area, add protocol conformance to types you don't own, add helpers to Array/String, add typed namespaces.

question: What is protocol-oriented programming and how is it different from class inheritance?
answer:
Protocol-oriented programming (POP) — a Swift idiom championed at WWDC 2015 — composes behavior via small protocols and default implementations rather than deep class hierarchies.

Why POP over class inheritance:
• Works with structs and enums, not just classes
• Multiple conformance — a type can adopt many protocols, sidestepping single-inheritance
• Composition over inheritance — features added in tiny chunks (Equatable, Hashable, Identifiable) and mixed at will
• Better testability — protocols become natural mock boundaries
• No fragile-base-class problem

When to keep inheritance: deep frameworks (UIView/NSObject), Cocoa/UIKit interop, override-based template methods.

question: Explain access control levels in Swift.
answer:
Five access levels, from most to least visible:

• open — accessible everywhere AND subclassable/overridable from any module. Class/method only.
• public — accessible from any module, but subclasses/overrides only inside the defining module.
• package (Swift 5.9+) — accessible from any module marked with the same package.
• internal (default) — accessible inside the current module only.
• fileprivate — accessible inside the same source file.
• private — accessible inside the enclosing declaration (and same-file extensions of it).

Access of a member is bounded by access of its container — a public method on an internal type is effectively internal.

question: What are property observers and lazy properties?
answer:
• willSet / didSet — property observers fired before / after the value changes. Cannot be combined with computed properties; only on stored properties of classes/structs.
• lazy — stored property whose initializer runs on first access. Must be `var` (because the value is mutated on first access). Not thread-safe by default.
• Computed property — no storage; getter (and optional setter) runs every access.

Use observers for side-effects on change (UI invalidation, analytics). Use lazy for expensive defaults you might not need. Use computed for derived values.
